description: SMTP is used to send email. In labs and CTFs, SMTP can reveal hostnames, mail domains, valid users, supported commands, relay behavior, and sometimes useful banners or misconfigurations.
why_prioritize: SMTP often exposes mail server banners, domain names, and supported commands that help identify the environment.
User enumeration may be possible through commands like VRFY or EXPN when they are enabled.
Mail domains and usernames discovered from SMTP can become useful when checking web logins, SSH, SMB, databases, or password reset flows.

enumeration_steps.title: Check the SMTP banner and version
enumeration_steps.reason: The banner can reveal the mail server product, hostname, operating system hints, or custom information.

enumeration_steps.title: List supported SMTP commands
enumeration_steps.reason: Supported commands can show whether user enumeration, authentication, STARTTLS, or other features are available.

enumeration_steps.title: Check user enumeration behavior
enumeration_steps.reason: Some SMTP servers reveal whether a username or mailbox exists through different responses.

enumeration_steps.title: Check relay behavior safely
enumeration_steps.reason: Open relay checks help identify whether the server accepts mail for external domains. This should be checked carefully in labs only.

next_steps: Add the domain and hostname to your notes.
Use the domain when checking DNS, web virtual hosts, and email address patterns.
Look for webmail, mail admin panels, or related MX records.
Connect the hostname to other services found in the scan.
Test a small, focused username list based on names found elsewhere in the lab.
Record confirmed usernames and the command that confirmed them.
Use valid usernames when checking web logins, SSH, SMB, RDP, or databases.
Compare response differences carefully before marking a user as valid.
Check whether TLS is required or optional.
Review certificate details for hostnames, domains, and organization names.
Use TLS-aware tools when continuing SMTP checks.
Take note of certificate names that may reveal other hosts.
Record supported authentication methods.
Check whether credentials found elsewhere might apply to mail login.
Look for webmail or mail client configuration files that mention the same service.
Avoid blind brute force; use gathered credentials and clear lab reasoning.
Document the exact Nmap result and the tested relay path.
Check whether the behavior is limited to internal domains or fully open.
Treat it as a configuration finding unless the lab provides a clear next step.
Do not send real external email from lab systems.

beginner_notes: SMTP is often useful for gathering usernames, domains, and hostnames before attempting login checks elsewhere.
Different SMTP response messages matter, so read them carefully instead of only looking for success or failure.
Use small targeted wordlists in labs. Blind guessing creates noise and usually wastes time.
Mail-related findings often connect back to DNS, webmail, password reset pages, and username formats.
